initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: The only concern is HttpApiUtils.CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN being read at class-load time, which makes it impossible to set the system property after the class is loaded (e.g., in test setup). While this is documented and provides a safe default, it is a minor initialization-coupling issue.

evidence: Test files found: BCQLParserTest, BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest, BlackLabDummyTest, BlackLabQueryExecutorTest, BlackLabSnippetParserTest, CollocateProfileComparatorTest, CollocateQueryHelperTest, ConlluConverterTest (11 new tests recently added), CqlUtilsTest, DependencySketchTest, GrammarConfigLoaderTest, HandlersTest, HttpApiUtilsTest, HybridAlgorithmTest, LogDiceCalculatorTest, LongIntHashMapTest, PatternSubstitutionTest, RadialPlotTest, RelationUtilsTest, RelationsValidationTest, SemanticFieldExplorerTest — 21 test classes.
BlackLabQueryExecutorTest is entirely skipped unless CONCEPT_SKETCH_TEST_INDEX env var is set — the main query executor has no unit tests that run in CI without a live index.

CollocateQueryHelper (920 LOC, core ranking/scoring logic) is tested via CollocateQueryHelperTest which covers extractCollocateLemma but not buildAndRankCollocates(), executeCollocateSearch(), or collectHits()/scoreHits().
SemanticFieldExplorerTest uses a StubExecutor — this is a well-designed unit test approach for the exploration logic.
ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers are tested via HandlersTest with a MockExchange — good handler-layer tests for 400/405 error cases.
WordSketchApiServer and the full HTTP routing layer have no integration tests verifying end-to-end response shapes for success paths (only error paths are covered in HandlersTest).
ConlluConverter now has 11 tests covering sentence counts, token counts, chunking, MWT filtering, empty-input edge cases — this is recent and good coverage.
GrammarConfigLoader is well-tested via GrammarConfigLoaderTest.
LogDiceCalculator, CqlUtils, LongIntHashMap, RelationUtils, BlackLabSnippetParser all have dedicated unit tests.
Missing: tests for buildAndRankCollocates() in CollocateQueryHelper — this is the core scoring method and has no direct test.
Missing: tests for ExplorationHandlers success paths (handleSemanticFieldExplore with valid params, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti) — only error-case handlers are tested.
Missing: integration test verifying that the full explore pipeline (ExplorationHandlers → SemanticFieldExplorer → CollocateProfileComparator) produces the correct JSON response shape.

QueryExecutor interface has 6 public methods; all are synchronous (Java, no async); parameter ordering is consistent: lemma, pattern/deprel, minLogDice, maxResults across findCollocations, executeDependencyPattern, executeDependencyPatternWithPos, executeSurfacePattern.
executeDependencyPattern and executeDependencyPatternWithPos differ only in a headPosConstraint parameter appended at the end — this is a standard Java overload pattern and is well-documented.

findCollocations vs executeSurfacePattern: both return List<WordSketchResult> but have different semantics (findCollocations prepends the lemma as a quoted token; executeSurfacePattern uses labeled positions). The distinction is documented in QueryExecutor Javadoc but the method names alone do not communicate the difference clearly.

REST API endpoints: GET /api/sketch/{lemma} and GET /api/sketch/{lemma}/dep both work, but /api/sketch/{lemma}/{relation} vs /api/sketch/{lemma}/dep/{deprel} uses path-based dispatch that is not documented in a machine-readable way (no OpenAPI, no route listing endpoint).
Exploration endpoints accept 'seeds' as the parameter name for both single-seed (?seeds=house) and multi-seed (?seeds=house,garden) — consistent naming.

GET /api/semantic-field takes 'seeds' but GET /api/semantic-field/examples takes 'adjective' and 'noun' separately — inconsistent: the comparison endpoint uses 'seeds' but the examples endpoint uses individual parameters. A developer familiar with the comparison endpoint expects 'seeds' on the examples endpoint.

GET /api/concordance/examples uses 'word1' and 'word2' while the semantic-field endpoints use 'seeds' for equivalent noun parameters — inconsistent naming for the same concept (headword/collocate noun).

POST /api/bcql has a body-JSON interface while all other endpoints use query parameters — this is intentional (documented: 'to avoid URL encoding issues') but creates a mixed sync/async-style inconsistency at the transport layer.
Parameter 'limit' is used in /api/bcql and /api/concordance/examples but 'top' is used in exploration endpoints — two different names for the same concept (max results count).
All public handler methods declare throws IOException — consistent error contract at the Java level.

WordSketchApiServer runs as a localhost-bound server: 'new InetSocketAddress(InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress(), port)' — all endpoints are only reachable on the local machine by design. This is the primary security boundary.
No authentication or authorisation layer exists: no API keys, no tokens, no session checks. All endpoints are effectively open to any local process.

All endpoints apply the same treatment (no auth) consistently — there is no pattern where some endpoints check permissions and others do not. The consistency is total, even if the choice is 'no auth for anyone'.

CORS is the only client-origin control: CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN restricts browser-based cross-origin access to the configured origin (default: localhost:3000). This is applied uniformly to all responses via sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendBinaryResponse, and sendOptionsResponse.

Input validation is applied on all endpoints: required parameters are checked (requireParam), numeric parameters are parsed with NumberFormatException handling, BCQL pattern length is capped at MAX_BCQL_PATTERN_LENGTH (1024 chars), request body size is capped at MAX_REQUEST_BODY_BYTES (65536).
There are no route handlers that skip input validation while siblings validate — validation consistency is high.
No permission strings or RLS patterns exist in this codebase (it is a local research tool, not a multi-tenant service).
The server binds to loopback only — no admin bypass or service-role escalation patterns to evaluate.
No audit logging exists, but for a local research tool this is not a significant gap.
POST /api/bcql executes arbitrary BCQL patterns against the local index — this is intentional and the index is read-only from the server's perspective, but there is no pattern-level access control.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The /api/bcql endpoint accepts arbitrary query patterns with no rate limiting or additional validation beyond length. For a loopback-only tool this is acceptable, but the pattern execution could be expensive and there is no per-request cost control.

strengths: Package decomposition broadly mirrors domain capabilities: api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils, collections, viz are all purposeful and non-overlapping
Dependency directions are correct in most directions: api depends on all subsystems; query, config, and exploration depend on model; collections is a true leaf
LongIntHashMap was properly relocated to a dedicated collections/ package rather than polluting utils/
Handler split (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers) keeps the api/ package focused without creating a god class
GrammarConfig extraction into a value object separate from GrammarConfigLoader demonstrates correct loader/data separation
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: A single structural cycle — model ↔ exploration — where model DTOs import graph-edge types from the exploration package while exploration classes import model DTOs in return; this is a two-class cluster problem, not a diffuse systemic smell.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The architecture is clearly intentional and mostly correct — packages are domain-aligned and dependency directions are clean in 7 of the 9 packages. However the bidirectional model ↔ exploration import creates a genuine package cycle: model.ExplorationResult and model.ComparisonResult import Edge and RelationEdgeType from the exploration package while the entire exploration package depends on model. This is a non-trivial structural defect because it couples the model package to a specific graph-edge representation that belongs in either the exploration or model package exclusively. The fix is straightforward (move Edge/RelationEdgeType to model/) but its absence keeps this from scoring as 'solid'.

strengths: Package names map directly to domain actions (exploration, query, indexer, config) rather than technical roles (services, managers, helpers)
WordSketchApiServer wires handlers together without implementing logic — the composition root is clean and navigable
GrammarConfigLoader/GrammarConfig separation keeps loading mechanics out of the data object used everywhere else
package-info.java documents the intentional mixed accessor convention rather than leaving it as an unexplained inconsistency
CLAUDE.md and ExploreOptions Javadoc show documentation kept in sync with runtime decisions
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Ownership blur at the model/exploration boundary: model package classes import exploration types and host graph-construction methods, preventing the model package from being a dependency-free leaf, and the getComparator() accessor exposes an exploration-internal collaborator to the API layer.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The top-level decomposition is clear and domain-aligned — a new engineer can navigate the codebase with confidence based on package names alone. The handler split and config-loader separation are particularly well-executed. What pulls the score down is the model package overreaching: it imports from exploration/ and implements buildEdges() — logic that belongs in exploration or a presentation layer. This is not a minor naming issue; it means the model package cannot be understood or tested in isolation. A 'solid but uneven' score is appropriate: most of the architecture is strong, but one persistent ownership violation prevents a higher score.

strengths: Naming conventions are uniformly followed: no snake_case methods, no inconsistent capitalisation, no style islands in any package
The `handle*` prefix is used consistently across all HTTP handler methods in all handler classes
Static utility class pattern (private constructor, all-static) is applied identically across HttpApiUtils, RelationUtils, CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator
Mixed record/class style in model/ is explicitly documented in package-info.java, converting a potential surprise into a communicated decision
convention_outlier.issue_character: Outliers are narrow and isolated: one unused record field (includeExamples), one undocumented naming asymmetry (Explore vs Exploration), and one private nested type that breaks the one-class-per-file norm — none systemic.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase achieves strong naming consistency across all packages: Java conventions, handler prefix, utility-class shape, and constructor-injection are all uniformly applied. No package deviates from the rest's structural norms. The documented model/ style mix and the noted Explore/Exploration asymmetry both carry explicit justification. What prevents a higher score is the unused includeExamples field in ExploreOptions (a dangling contract in an otherwise precise codebase) and the single private-nested-type outlier (ExecutedSketch). These are minor but genuine violations of the otherwise consistent pattern.

strengths: IOException is used uniformly as the checked boundary type across the entire query layer — no mix of checked/unchecked or multiple competing exception types
All BlackLab exception wrapping preserves the original cause via chained exceptions — context is not lost at the library boundary
API handlers correctly distinguish 400 (bad input) from 500 (execution failure) with separate catch blocks — the recent fix for HTTP error misclassification is reflected
GrammarConfigLoader uses targeted IOException messages that identify the specific config error by field/index — actionable error context for operators
error_consistency.issue_character: Two structural inconsistencies: a null-return error channel in handlers that runs parallel to the exception channel used elsewhere, and a silent swallow of URL-decode failures in parseQueryParams; both are contained to the API layer but affect predictability.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The query and config layers have strong, consistent error handling: IOException as the single checked type, cause-chained wrapping at library boundaries, and specific messages throughout. The API layer introduces two inconsistencies that pull the score down: parseQueryParams silently drops malformed query parameters (callers can't distinguish 'param missing' from 'param malformed'), and the null-return pattern used by requireParam/resolveRelationConfig/resolveExploreParams runs as a parallel non-exception error channel alongside the IOException approach used everywhere else. Neither is catastrophic, but they create predictability debt for developers extending the handler layer.

strengths: QueryExecutor's four method names precisely distinguish four different execution semantics, preventing the vocabulary collapse that commonly occurs when similar-but-distinct operations exist
Domain-vocabulary names throughout: exploreByPattern, exploreMultiSeed, compareCollocateProfiles, fetchExamples — these read as domain sentences
The 'handle*' prefix convention applied consistently to all HTTP handler methods makes the API entry points immediately identifiable
findLabelTokenIndex correctly renamed from findLabelPosition, and LEMMA_ATTR_RELAXED renamed from LEMMA_ATTR_ANY — recent improvements show active naming hygiene
naming_quality.issue_character: A small cluster of naming mismatches — one method that performs construction but is named as a getter, one ambiguous 'forPrefix' helper name, and an inconsistent build/format vocabulary in a single class — rather than any systemic pattern.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Naming quality across the codebase is above average: domain terms are used accurately, the most-trafficked interfaces have precise discriminating names, and recent naming fixes demonstrate intentional improvement. The defects are narrow: getFullPattern() misrepresents a construction operation as a retrieval, posGroupForPrefix communicates neither its input (a CQL constraint) nor its output (a PosGroup) clearly, and the build/format prefix mixture in ExplorationHandlers creates a small vocabulary inconsistency. These are genuine naming issues but confined to a few files, not systemic across the codebase.

strengths: CqlUtils provides well-leveraged primitives: splitCqlTokens, substituteAtPosition, and escapeForRegex are each used in 2-3 callers and eliminate real duplication
GrammarConfigLoader/GrammarConfig separation cleanly isolates file-IO from all data consumers — clear abstraction pay-off
HttpApiUtils consolidates CORS-header and content-type boilerplate for all handlers with zero redundancy — the helper earns its existence
CollocateQueryHelper is package-private, preventing abstraction leakage to unintended consumers
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: A cluster of low-leverage indirections: a single-implementation interface (QueryExecutor), a pass-through accessor (getComparator), a 1-line wrapper method (substituteHeadword), a dead field in an options record (includeExamples), and graph-construction logic embedded in data DTOs — each individually minor but together indicating a pattern of adding abstraction layers without verifying they pay off.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase has several genuine high-leverage abstractions (CqlUtils, GrammarConfigLoader/Config split, HttpApiUtils) that justify their existence. However it also carries a recurring pattern of thin or unearned indirections: getComparator() exposes an internal object; substituteHeadword delegates to CqlUtils with no additional semantics; QueryExecutor has one concrete implementation; ExploreOptions carries a field that does nothing; and HTTP response bodies are assembled via scattered Map.put() calls rather than typed structures. At the 'significant drag' threshold, this sits above 60 because the core utilities are fit, but the unearned indirections are numerous enough to affect maintainability.

strengths: The dependency set is minimal: 8 dependencies (3 test-scoped) for a tool with corpus indexing, HTTP server, JSON API, and logging — no unnecessary libraries pulled in
No multiple libraries for the same purpose: single JSON library (fastjson2), single logging implementation (logback), single corpus engine (BlackLab)
jspecify used at its current stable release (1.0.0) — the newest available
The pom.xml explicitly documents the BlackLab repo risk with a note about mitigation options — the risk is acknowledged rather than hidden
dependency_health.issue_character: Two isolated dependency hygiene issues: an unreliable repository source for the most critical dependency, and an outdated version of the JSON library with known CVE exposure — neither caused by structural coupling choices.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency set is lean and well-chosen — no redundant libraries, no heavy-for-light-use pulls, no visible version conflicts. The codebase loses points for two hygiene issues: the BlackLab artifacts are sourced from a raw GitHub URL that has no availability guarantees (a build infrastructure risk that could cause reproducibility failures in CI), and fastjson2 is pinned at 2.0.25 which is significantly behind the current release line and carries known deserialization CVEs. These are not structural coupling problems but they are real dependency health concerns.

strengths: Four-phase decomposition in SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern with named private methods for each phase makes the algorithm traceable at a glance
Guard clauses at the top of BlackLabQueryExecutor methods eliminate nesting — control flow is direct and branches are necessary
collectFrequenciesFromGroups extraction with a Function parameter correctly unifies three near-identical loops without over-abstracting
LongIntHashMap implementation is precise: sentinel is documented, rehash threshold is enforced, the API matches exactly what callers need
low_level_elegance.issue_character: A few methods in the API layer (buildExploreResponseBody, formatAdjectiveProfile, handleBlackLabIndexCommand) are monolithic assemblers that do several things in sequence without sub-extraction, while the algorithmic layer (SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateQueryHelper) is well-decomposed — the elegance is uneven across layers.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The core algorithmic code (exploration phases, collocate ranking pipeline, CQL utilities) is clean, well-decomposed, and direct. The API response-building layer is noticeably less refined: buildExploreResponseBody assembles three different list structures and their counts in one 40-line method, formatAdjectiveProfile packs a classification judgment and 9 map entries into 25 imperative lines, and handleBlackLabIndexCommand mixes argument parsing and multi-step orchestration without sub-extraction. The asymmetry between layers places this at 'solid in the algorithmic core, significant drag in the API layer'.

strengths: parseBaseExploreRequest extraction unifies the HTTP-to-params translation across all four exploration handlers — one parsing seam, one translation point
resolveRelationConfig and resolveExploreParams correctly localize validation at the handler boundary before any domain object is constructed
RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias localises all alias → config ID translations in a single map, preventing alias-resolution logic from leaking into domain code
SemanticFieldExplorer accepts strongly typed RelationConfig and ExploreOptions at its input boundary — not raw strings or query parameter maps
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Boundary crossings are generally correct but several carry unnecessary seam noise: the graph-edge data makes three hops across two packages, the comparison comparator is exposed rather than delegated, and ExploreOptions crosses the handler-explorer seam carrying a field that is never consumed.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The handler-to-domain boundary is well-designed: typed option objects, localised validation, and single-point alias resolution are all in place. However the return path from domain to HTTP response is less refined: the edge-building pathway fragments across buildEdges (model), toMap (exploration), and the handler's stream collect; the comparison boundary requires callers to understand the explorer's internal comparator; and ExploreOptions carries a dead contract field (includeExamples) across the seam. These are not catastrophic but they add glue-code entropy at boundaries that should be clean. The score reflects 'mostly correct intent, unfinished execution at return boundaries'.

strengths: Algorithmic Javadoc comments (SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateQueryHelper phase comments, GrammarConfigLoader structure doc) explain WHY and HOW, not just WHAT — genuine documentation rather than restatement
LogDiceCalculator has no defensive sprawl: null inputs are handled via a documented sentinel (UNCOMPUTABLE = NaN), the formula is direct, and the long overload is a clean delegation
RelationConfig's TODO comments for NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT and ADJECTIVE_PATTERN explicitly identify what needs to change and why — actionable technical debt markers, not filler
CqlUtils methods have example-based Javadoc that demonstrates input/output — this is the right use of documentation for non-trivial parsing utilities
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: A small cluster of AI-hallmark patterns — one dead field, one pass-through wrapper, one redundant toString, and one inconsistent null-check pattern — rather than systemic boilerplate; the deliberate code is clearly distinguishable from the generated-style artifacts.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase shows clear intentionality in its core algorithmic and documentation choices: class Javadocs explain non-obvious decisions, utility methods earn their existence, and TODOs are actionable. The AI-debt markers are confined to a small set of identified artifacts: the includeExamples dead field, the substituteHeadword 1-line wrapper, the redundant RelationEdgeType.toString(), and the copy-paste null-guard pattern in BlackLabQueryExecutor. None of these are serious defects, but their co-occurrence in an otherwise deliberate codebase suggests AI-assisted code generation that was not fully reviewed. The score reflects 'above average, recognizable AI debris cluster without systemic contamination'.

strengths: The recent model-reorg is complete: all relocated types (RelationType, PosGroup, ExploreOptions, Edge, RelationEdgeType, LongIntHashMap, GrammarConfig) have been moved and there are no stale import sites left behind.
No @Deprecated or DEPRECATED-annotated symbols remain in production code — deprecated-but-called code is not a problem here.
The tag=/xpos= backward-compat fallback in posGroupFromConstraint() is clearly documented with an inline comment explaining its purpose.
Package-info.java in model/ documents the naming-convention asymmetry between record and non-record types, preventing confusion about mixed styles.
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The only residual migration concerns are two acknowledged TODOs (config-injection for hardcoded POS patterns) and a minimally-documented legacy fallback path — all are small, isolated, and intentional rather than systemic neglect.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase scores solidly above the 80 ('solid but uneven') anchor. The major structural migration (package reorg) is finished and clean. The remaining items are two forward-looking TODOs that are correctly scoped and a tested-but-not-unit-tested compatibility shim; neither constitutes abandoned migration work. The score stops short of 95 because the two TODOs lack tracking tickets or target dates, leaving a small ambiguity about their lifecycle.

strengths: All packages are focused and below the 10-file overload threshold; no mixed-concern flat directory issues.
The recent model-reorg correctly separated enum types (RelationType, PosGroup) and value objects (ExploreOptions, GrammarConfig) into purpose-aligned packages.
Package naming is unambiguous: api, config, exploration, model, query, utils, indexer/blacklab all reflect contained responsibilities.
No straggler root-level files with low fan-in — Main.java is genuinely the CLI entry point.
package_organization.issue_character: Issues are mild placement ambiguities at package boundaries (model importing exploration types, Edge/RelationEdgeType belonging to graph output), not structural fragmentation or coupling violations.
package_organization.score_rationale: The layout is clearly navigable and recently improved by the model-reorg. The score falls in the 80-85 range because of one soft boundary violation: ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult in model/ import Edge and RelationEdgeType from exploration/, which inverts the expected model→exploration direction. This is a minor but measurable coupling direction issue. No flat-dir overloads, no import-affinity mismatches above 60%, and no coupling-direction failures between sibling directories push the score above 80.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface documents all method contracts including error types and parameter semantics.
HttpApiUtils.requireParam() @Nullable annotation + Javadoc enforces caller null-check discipline explicitly.
LogDiceCalculator uses a named sentinel (UNCOMPUTABLE) rather than a magic value, and documents it.
GrammarConfigLoader correctly separates load() (file-path entry) from fromReader() (test/streaming entry), and both have complete Javadoc.
contract_coherence.issue_character: Issues are concentrated around implicit 'null means error-already-sent' contracts in HTTP handlers and undocumented fallback behaviour in internal helpers, rather than wrong return types or signature lies.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase sits between 75 and 80. Public interfaces and data classes have honest, complete contracts. The weaker areas are the implicit handler-layer contracts (null-from-resolve = 400-already-sent, posMap null-passthrough) which are correct but require reading the implementation to understand. These are real contract-coherence gaps but isolated to the HTTP handler layer and one internal helper — they do not represent a systemic pattern across the whole codebase.

strengths: Guard clauses are used consistently throughout handlers: early returns on null, empty string, or invalid state before the main logic.
No identical if/else branches or dead code after unconditional returns observed across the reviewed files.
Enum switch expressions use exhaustive coverage with throw-on-default rather than silent fall-through.
Integer overflow fix in scoreHits() is correct and the cast-to-long strategy is clear.
logic_clarity.issue_character: Issues are isolated to one unusual control-flow pattern in ConlluConverter (nested try/finally + try-with-resources writer close) that is correct but requires extra reading to verify — not a systemic logic problem.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The logic is generally clear with consistent guard-clause style and no duplicate branches. The ConlluConverter writer-close pattern (try-with-resources inside finally, increment after) is the most confusing flow in the codebase; it is correct but a developer maintaining it must trace the control flow carefully. This, plus the boolean-return requireMethod() that relies on caller discipline, keeps the score around 82 rather than higher. There are no always-true/always-false conditions or dead code paths.

strengths: Extensive use of records for DTOs provides compile-time field-type enforcement across the data layer.
@Nullable from jspecify is applied consistently to the key nullable return paths in the API handler layer.
Sealed interface ConcordanceResult with permits enforces exhaustive handling of result subtypes.
Enum types (RelationType, PosGroup, RelationEdgeType) are used everywhere instead of raw strings for discriminated values.
type_safety.issue_character: Issues are concentrated around missing @Nullable annotations on a small number of methods that can return null (getFullPattern, getConfigPath), creating gaps where the type system does not reflect the actual nullability contract.
type_safety.score_rationale: The score sits around 75: the core data layer is well-typed using records and sealed interfaces. The gaps are real: RelationConfig.getFullPattern() can return null but is not annotated @Nullable, and GrammarConfig.getConfigPath() returns Path without @Nullable despite the null case. These are used at call sites without null checks. The codebase is well above the 60 anchor (significant drag) because the domain model types are sound, but the nullable-contract gaps in config types prevent reaching 85.

strengths: iterateRelations() in SketchHandlers is a well-designed extraction: a callback-based loop that removes two near-identical iteration blocks.
GrammarConfigLoader is cleanly decomposed into small, named private helpers with single responsibilities.
WordSketchApiServer.registerHandler() abstracts CORS and method enforcement into one place for all 11 routes.
The two-phase BCQL pipeline in CollocateQueryHelper (collectHits / scoreHits) is clearly named and separated.
design_coherence.issue_character: The primary design-coherence issue is CollocateQueryHelper accumulating five distinct concerns (frequency lookup, grouped search, collocate ranking, hit collection, hit scoring) in one class — it is coherent within each phase but the phases together make the class responsible for too much.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The score sits around 79: the recent sketch-dedup refactor (iterateRelations, parseBaseExploreRequest) cleanly removed duplication. The remaining structural issue is CollocateQueryHelper's broad responsibility scope — it is not a god class (each phase is delegated cleanly) but splitting it into CollocateSearchHelper and BcqlScoringHelper would improve navigability. The boilerplate duplication flagged by the mechanical detector is low-severity on inspection: the two methods have different responsibilities and the structural similarity does not represent a missed extraction opportunity.

strengths: The entire object graph is constructed explicitly in Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand() with no hidden singletons or order-dependent module-level state.
GrammarConfigLoader is fully stateless and side-effect-free — loading is always explicit.
Constructor injection is used throughout: WordSketchApiServer, SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, SemanticFieldExplorer all receive their dependencies via constructor.
The AtomicReference shutdown-hook pattern correctly handles the ordering between hook registration and server construction.
initialization_coupling.issue_character: The only initialization-coupling issue is the single System.getProperty() call at class-load time in HttpApiUtils, which is a minor but real constraint on test flexibility.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: This codebase scores well above the 80 anchor for initialization coupling. The dependency graph is explicit, constructor-injected, and has no hidden singletons or import-time side effects beyond the documented CORS property read. The CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN static final is the only thing preventing a higher score: it makes the CORS origin impossible to change after class loading, which affects test isolation. This is a small, localized concern.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorerTest uses a stub executor, enabling pure unit tests of exploration logic without a live index.
ConlluConverter now has 11 targeted tests covering all edge cases (MWT, empty input, chunking boundaries).
HandlersTest tests HTTP error cases (400, 405) via MockExchange without starting a real server.
Pure utility classes (LogDiceCalculator, CqlUtils, LongIntHashMap, BlackLabSnippetParser) all have dedicated unit tests.
test_strategy.issue_character: The gap is not in test tooling or test fragility but in coverage of the core query-ranking pipeline: CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates() and the BCQL scoring pipeline (collectHits/scoreHits) have no direct tests, and exploration handler success paths are untested.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The score sits at 65 — the codebase has a reasonable test framework and good coverage of utility and conversion code, but the highest-complexity, highest-risk paths (collocate ranking, BCQL scoring, exploration handler success responses) lack direct tests. BlackLabQueryExecutorTest is entirely environment-gated, meaning the main query executor is practically untested in CI. This is a significant drag — the 60 anchor ('significant drag on correctness confidence') is appropriate here, but the well-tested utilities and recently-added ConlluConverter tests push it slightly above.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface uses consistent parameter ordering (lemma, pattern, minLogDice, maxResults) across all overloads.
Exploration endpoints consistently use 'seeds' as the multi-word parameter name.
All handler methods return JSON with a 'status: ok' envelope — consistent response shape.
The POST vs GET split for /api/bcql is explicitly documented with a rationale comment.
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: Issues are parameter-naming inconsistencies across related endpoints (limit vs top, word1/word2 vs seeds/adjective/noun) and one semantic inconsistency (findCollocations vs executeSurfacePattern with near-identical signatures but different behaviours).
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The score is around 73: the Java API layer (QueryExecutor interface) is coherent and consistent. The HTTP REST surface has three naming inconsistencies (limit/top, word1/word2/seeds/adjective/noun) that a developer using the API would notice when switching between endpoints. These are real usability gaps but not critical bugs. The codebase is well above 60 because the response envelope shape is consistent and all endpoints are documented in WordSketchApiServer Javadoc.

strengths: The loopback-only binding is a strong network-level access control applied uniformly to all endpoints.
CORS headers are applied consistently to every response type (JSON, binary, error, options).
Input validation (required params, numeric parsing, length limits) is applied uniformly across all endpoints with no siblings that skip it.
No mixed-trust-boundary pattern: all endpoints have the same trust model.
authorization_consistency.issue_character: There is no access control inconsistency because there is no access control at all — the codebase is a consistent, fully-open local tool. The only concern is the /api/bcql pattern execution accepting arbitrary queries without rate limiting.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The score is around 82 for a local research tool. The rubric asks about consistency of applied auth patterns, not about the presence or absence of auth. Within the tool's stated scope (localhost, research), all endpoints are treated uniformly — no siblings differ. The loopback binding and CORS are the security perimeter and they are applied consistently. The score is docked slightly for the lack of rate limiting or cost control on the arbitrary-pattern BCQL endpoint, which is a mild inconsistency in that other endpoints have clear cost-bounding mechanisms (max results, body size limit) but BCQL has only a length limit.

issues.summary: Bidirectional package cycle: model.ExplorationResult/ComparisonResult import from exploration/ while all of exploration/ imports from model/
issues.suggestion: Move Edge.java and RelationEdgeType.java from exploration/ to model/. Update all imports. This breaks the cycle with minimal collateral change and places graph-edge value types where model-layer DTOs can import them without a circular dependency.
evidence: ExplorationResult.java: 'import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.exploration.Edge; import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.exploration.RelationEdgeType;'
ComparisonResult.java: same two imports from exploration package
SemanticFieldExplorer imports model.ExplorationResult, model.CoreCollocate, model.DiscoveredNoun, model.PosGroup, model.QueryResults

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator hardcode English POS tag patterns instead of deriving them from RelationConfig, violating the config-driven boundary
